title: Intracranial Hemorrhage
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Neurology, Neurosurgery
objectives: Classify ICH by location and etiology
Apply initial stabilization and BP management

vignette.text:
A 72-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with sudden left-sided weakness and headache. He was found on the floor by his wife 1 hour ago. His last known INR was 3.2.

Past Medical History: Atrial fibrillation, hypertension, prior stroke

Medications: Warfarin, metoprolol, lisinopril

physicalExam.neuro: GCS 12 (E3V4M5), left hemiparesis 2/5, left facial droop, leftward gaze deviation, pupils equal and reactive

questions.stem: CT shows right basal ganglia hemorrhage with surrounding edema.
questions.question: How is intracranial hemorrhage classified?

options.text: ICH CLASSIFICATION: BY LOCATION: DEEP/BASAL GANGLIA (most common - 50%): Putamen, thalamus, caudate; Usually hypertensive; LOBAR (30%): Cerebral cortex; More often CAA, AVM, tumor, or coagulopathy; CEREBELLAR (10%): Posterior fossa - high surgical urgency; BRAINSTEM/PONTINE (10%): Often devastating, poor prognosis; BY ETIOLOGY: PRIMARY (80-85%): Hypertension (chronic, lipohyalinosis); Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (elderly, lobar); SECONDARY (15-20%): Anticoagulation (like this patient); Vascular malformation (AVM, aneurysm); Tumor; Coagulopathy; Drug-induced (cocaine, amphetamines); Hemorrhagic conversion of stroke; Trauma
Lobar hemorrhage is most common
All ICH is from hypertension
Anticoagulation rarely causes ICH

questions.explanation: ICH Classification: BY LOCATION - Deep/basal ganglia most common (50%, usually hypertensive), lobar (30%, often CAA/AVM/coagulopathy), cerebellar (10%, surgical urgency), brainstem (10%, poor prognosis). BY ETIOLOGY - Primary (80-85%): hypertension, CAA. Secondary (15-20%): anticoagulation (this patient), AVM, tumor, drugs, hemorrhagic transformation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Intracranial Hemorrhage. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Powers WJ, et al. Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the initial management of ICH?

options.text: Routine seizure prophylaxis recommended
No BP lowering in acute ICH
FFP is preferred for warfarin reversal

ICH INITIAL MANAGEMENT: AIRWAY: Intubate if GCS ≤8, cannot protect airway, or rapidly declining; BLOOD PRESSURE: Target SBP 140 mmHg (AHA 2022); Safe to lower to 140 if presenting 150-220; Use IV agents: Nicardipine, labetalol, clevidipine; Avoid aggressive lowering if SBP >220 (risk of ischemia); REVERSE ANTICOAGULATION (urgent in this patient): INR 3.4 on warfarin = needs immediate reversal; 4-factor PCC 25-50 units/kg (preferred); + Vitamin K 10mg IV; Goal: INR ≤1.4 within 4 hours; GLUCOSE: Maintain 140-180 mg/dL; TEMPERATURE: Treat fever aggressively; POSITIONING: Head of bed 30°; SEIZURE: Prophylaxis controversial - not routinely recommended; Treat if clinical seizure

questions.explanation: ICH Initial Management: AIRWAY if GCS ≤8. BP target SBP 140 (safe if presenting 150-220). REVERSE anticoagulation urgently: 4-factor PCC 25-50 units/kg (preferred over FFP) + Vitamin K 10mg IV, goal INR ≤1.4 within 4h. Maintain glucose 140-180, treat fever, HOB 30°. Seizure prophylaxis NOT routinely recommended.

questions.question: How do you reverse different anticoagulants in ICH?

options.text: FFP is preferred over PCC for warfarin

ANTICOAGULATION REVERSAL IN ICH: WARFARIN: 4-factor PCC 25-50 units/kg IV (onset minutes); + Vitamin K 10mg IV (sustained effect); Goal INR ≤1.4 within 4 hours; Avoid FFP alone (slow, volume, incomplete reversal); DABIGATRAN (direct thrombin inhibitor): Idarucizumab 5g IV (specific reversal agent); RIVAROXABAN/APIXABAN/EDOXABAN (Factor Xa inhibitors): Andexanet alfa (if available); OR 4-factor PCC 50 units/kg; HEPARIN: Protamine sulfate 1mg per 100 units heparin; LMWH: Protamine (partial reversal only); ANTIPLATELET: Platelet transfusion controversial; DDAVP 0.3 mcg/kg may help; Check with hematology
Vitamin K alone is sufficient acutely
Same reversal for all DOACs

questions.explanation: Anticoagulation Reversal: WARFARIN - 4-factor PCC 25-50 U/kg (preferred) + Vitamin K 10mg IV, goal INR ≤1.4 in 4h. DABIGATRAN - Idarucizumab 5g IV. FACTOR Xa INHIBITORS - Andexanet alfa or PCC 50 U/kg. HEPARIN - Protamine. ANTIPLATELET - Platelet transfusion controversial, DDAVP may help.

questions.question: When is surgery indicated for ICH?

options.text: Cerebellar hemorrhage can be observed
All ICH >30mL needs surgery
Deep hemorrhages benefit most from surgery

SURGICAL INDICATIONS IN ICH: CEREBELLAR HEMORRHAGE (clearest indication): >3 cm diameter; Brainstem compression; Hydrocephalus; Declining neurologic status; SUPRATENTORIAL ICH (controversial): Lobar hemorrhage >30mL within 1cm of surface; Declining status from mass effect; Young patient with salvageable territory; GENERALLY NOT INDICATED: Deep hemorrhage (basal ganglia, thalamus); Small hemorrhage (<10mL); GCS ≤4 with absent brainstem reflexes (poor prognosis); HYDROCEPHALUS: EVD for IVH with hydrocephalus; MINIMALLY INVASIVE SURGERY: Emerging - stereotactic aspiration; May reduce mortality in selected patients

questions.explanation: Surgical Indications: CEREBELLAR ICH (clearest indication) - >3cm, brainstem compression, hydrocephalus, declining status. SUPRATENTORIAL controversial but consider for lobar >30mL near surface with mass effect. NOT indicated for deep hemorrhage, small bleeds, or GCS ≤4 with absent brainstem reflexes. EVD for hydrocephalus with IVH.

questions.question: How do you assess prognosis in ICH?

options.text: ICH SCORE (0-6 points): GCS 3-4: 2 points; GCS 5-12: 1 point; GCS 13-15: 0 points; ICH volume ≥30mL: 1 point; IVH present: 1 point; Infratentorial origin: 1 point; Age ≥80: 1 point; 30-DAY MORTALITY BY SCORE: 0: 0%; 1: 13%; 2: 26%; 3: 72%; 4: 97%; 5-6: 100%; POOR PROGNOSTIC FACTORS: Large volume; Low GCS; IVH extension; Infratentorial location; Anticoagulation-associated; Hematoma expansion; IMPORTANT: Early withdrawal of care based on perceived prognosis may become self-fulfilling; Full treatment should continue early
ICH volume doesn't predict outcome
Age is not prognostic
Location doesn't affect prognosis

questions.explanation: ICH Score (0-6): GCS (0-2 pts), volume ≥30mL (1 pt), IVH (1 pt), infratentorial (1 pt), age ≥80 (1 pt). 30-day mortality: Score 0=0%, 3=72%, 5-6=100%. Poor factors: large volume, low GCS, IVH, infratentorial, anticoagulation, hematoma expansion. IMPORTANT: Avoid early withdrawal based solely on perceived prognosis - may be self-fulfilling.

D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is ICH SCORE (0-6 points): GCS 3-4: 2 points; GCS 5-12: 1 point; GCS 13-15: 0 points; ICH volume ≥30mL: 1 point; IVH present: 1 point; Infratentorial origin: 1 point; Age ≥80: 1 point; 30-DAY MORTALITY BY SCORE: 0: 0%; 1: 13%; 2: 26%; 3: 72%; 4: 97%; 5-6: 100%; POOR PROGNOSTIC FACTORS: Large volume; Low GCS; IVH extension; Infratentorial location; Anticoagulation-associated; Hematoma expansion; IMPORTANT: Early withdrawal of care based on perceived prognosis may become self-fulfilling; Full treatment should continue early because ICH Score (0-6): GCS (0-2 pts), volume ≥30mL (1 pt), IVH (1 pt), infratentorial (1 pt), age ≥80 (1 pt). 30-day mortality

questions.question: What is the complete management?

options.text: FFP alone for warfarin reversal

Complete approach: (1) STABILIZE: Airway (GCS 12, monitor closely), BP target 140 with nicardipine/labetalol; (2) REVERSE ANTICOAGULATION URGENTLY: 4-factor PCC 25-50 U/kg IV STAT; Vitamin K 10mg IV; Goal INR ≤1.4 within 4 hours; Recheck INR in 30-60 min; (3) NEUROSURGERY CONSULT: Basal ganglia = generally no surgery; But discuss given anticoagulation association; EVD if hydrocephalus develops; (4) ICU ADMISSION: Neuro checks q1h; Repeat CT in 6h or if decline; (5) SUPPORTIVE: HOB 30°, glucose control, fever prevention; DVT prophylaxis (pneumatic, then chemical once stable); (6) PROGNOSTIC DISCUSSION: Calculate ICH score, but avoid premature withdrawal; (7) LONG-TERM: Address anticoagulation resumption timing (usually weeks later if needed)
Immediate surgery for all ICH
Observe without anticoagulation reversal

questions.explanation: Complete Management: Stabilize (airway, BP to 140). REVERSE anticoagulation urgently with 4-factor PCC + Vitamin K, goal INR ≤1.4 in 4h. Neurosurgery consult (basal ganglia usually non-operative but discuss). ICU with frequent neuro checks and repeat imaging. Supportive care, DVT prophylaxis. Prognostic discussion but avoid premature withdrawal. Address long-term anticoagulation decisions later.

sources.text: StatPearls: Intracranial Hemorrhage. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Powers WJ, et al. Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected intracranial hemorrhage?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in intracranial hemorrhage. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in intracranial hemorrhage. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in intracranial hemorrhage. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

B.misconception: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
B.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
C.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of intracranial hemorrhage. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

B.misconception: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
B.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
C.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.
D.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: When managing intracranial hemorrhage, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

B.misconception: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.
B.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
C.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to Intracranial Hemorrhage including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for Intracranial Hemorrhage.

imaging_reasoning.order_reasoning: Non-contrast CT >95% sensitive in first 6h; LP if CT negative but high suspicion; CTA for aneurysm
imaging_reasoning.expected_findings: Hyperdense blood in basal cisterns, sylvian fissure, interhemispheric fissure
imaging_reasoning.management_impact: Fisher grade predicts vasospasm; aneurysm location determines clip vs coil; Hunt-Hess guides prognosis
